data.title: Broadcom: Entering A New Stage Of Growth (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Broadcom (AVGO) will be going into q4’25 in a position of strength following a robust earnings season for the major cloud services providers (CSPs). With growing interest by the major AI developers, Anthropic (ANTHRO) and OpenAI (OPENAI), Broadcom may realize a significant uplift in revenue in the coming quarters. As a result of the optimistic outlook, I am reiterating my "Strong Buy" rating for AVGO shares with a price target of $528/share at 29.86x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Broadcom is set to report q4’25 earnings on Dec. 11, 2025, after market close. Analysts have revised Broadcom EPS estimates up 27x and down 3x in the last 90 days, suggesting an optimistic outlook for the coming earnings release.

You can review my post-q3’25 earnings coverage of Broadcom here:

Broadcom: AI Infrastructure Spend Set To Drive Significant Growth

Investment Thesis

The q3’25 earnings season among the CSPs was extremely constructive for the AI chip space, particularly as each of the CSPs signaled a significant uplift in capital spend for the coming fiscal year. Growth in capital deployment isn’t an empty promise either; the increased capital outlay among CSPs is largely driven by the growing backlogs for cloud services, which now sit at an aggregate $1.21 trillion. As a result of the growing demand for cloud services, I am now forecasting an aggregate capital outlay of $454 billion for calendar year 2026, up from an estimated $368.5 billion in 2025.

One factor that will shift the attention towards chipmakers like Broadcom is the verbiage used by major CSPs like Microsoft (MSFT) in the quarter, suggesting that capital spending going forward will largely be tied to building out compute capacity or investing in GPUs, CPUs, XPUs, and networking & data center interconnect equipment.

Microsoft isn’t alone in the matter; peer CSPs like Alphabet (GOOG) will be investing in large-scale data centers in the coming years to support increasing investments made by model developers like Anthropic & OpenAI. Valued at roughly $50 billion, Alphabet will be supplying 1mm Ironwood TPUs to support a 1GW data center facility for Anthropic as part of a cloud hosting agreement. In addition to this, Alphabet may be set to scale Ironwood beyond previous TPU iterations, as the chips are designed to compete at a high level with Nvidia’s leading GPUs. As part of this, Alphabet is currently in talks with Meta Platforms (META) to deploy its TPUs as Meta invests in expanding its data center footprint by a significant degree. Accordingly, Meta has plans to invest $600 billion through 2028 to expand its data center footprint to support its Superintelligence Labs. If Meta seeks to leverage Alphabet’s TPUs in these developments, Broadcom may realize a substantial uplift in the coming years for its custom silicon.

Amazon (AMZN) has also signed a similar agreement for supplying 1mm chips for Anthropic as the AI developer expands its support across multiple infrastructure platforms. Amazon also signed a multi-year agreement with OpenAI to support tens of millions of GPUs in a deal valued at $38 billion.

In q4’25, Broadcom signed a deal directly with OpenAI to co-develop and supply 10GW of custom AI accelerators. The deal involves both custom silicon and networking solutions, all packaged in a server rack to be deployed for OpenAI. The deal is expected to commence in 2h26 and continue through the end of 2029. Using napkin math to value the deal based on Alphabet’s $50 billion deal for 1GW of capacity, Broadcom’s deal could be valued at upwards of $500 billion, though this will likely involve data center hosting, power, non-IT infrastructure like power distribution, and other factors, rather than purely infrastructure provided by Broadcom.

Advanced Micro Devices’ (AMD) deal for 6GW of compute capacity is expected to generate “tens of billions of dollars in revenue for AMD,” suggesting Broadcom’s portion of the deal may be more aligned with AMD’s OpenAI agreement. With the presumption that Broadcom may be in a position to charge a premium over AMD’s GPU supply agreement given that Broadcom’s chips will be purpose-built for OpenAI, Broadcom’s chip deal could provide more revenue on a per-gigawatt basis. Considering the additional networking infrastructure going into the server racks, Broadcom’s deal could be well north of $100 billion.

Financial Forecast

Looking ahead to eq4’25, I’m forecasting Broadcom to generate $17.7 billion in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $1.88/share. I believe the catalyst from the q4’25 earnings call will be forward guidance for eFY26, particularly as it relates to the growth trajectory among its custom silicon deals with the CSPs as well as its deal directly with OpenAI. I believe analysts will be critical of the economies of scale factor and how these deals may play both top-line growth and incremental expansion at the operating margin.

Risks Related to Broadcom

Bull Case

Broadcom is quickly becoming a central competitor to Nvidia as CSPs and AI developers seek to create more purpose-built infrastructure to facilitate AI application development. With the combination of custom silicon and networking infrastructure, Broadcom may be well positioned to realize substantial tailwinds as an integrated rack-scale solution.

Bear Case

Concerns are mounting relating to the profitability of AI applications, particularly as OpenAI seeks to accelerate development through substantial upfront investments for AI infrastructure. If investments were to slow down or if financing were to dry up, Broadcom may be left exposed to substantial risk of lost revenue.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

AVGO shares are currently trading at 48.92x EV/aEBITDA, a relative premium when considering shares’ historical trading range. Given the significant uplift in revenue Broadcom may experience in the coming years, I’m expecting operations to catch up to the market capitalization, narrowing the premium over time. Considering the timing of the expected revenue uplift, AVGO shares will likely appear relatively expensive for the next 2-3 quarters.

Given the strong growth forecast, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating for AVGO shares with a price target of $528/share at 29.86x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: Kroger: This Time I Disagree With The Strategy (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Two headwinds are hitting retailers: sticky inflation of 3% and cautious and value-seeking behaviors from lower-income cohorts. Last quarter, we saw that Kroger (KR) was able to cut prices and expand margins at the same time, thus making me state that the grocery retailer was delivering a masterclass to its peers, investing in price to win in volume. It may be easy to say, but it is hard to achieve. In fact, the company divested from its pharmacy business and leaned more toward private labels and focused on building a larger online scale. After Q2, we know that the company increased its guidance and expected FY sales to grow by at least 2.7% (ex-fuel). Traffic was improving, private label items gained market share, and the online channel was actually increasing sales, rather than partly cannibalizing them. In particular, we heard from Kroger's management that consumers visiting its stores were indeed trading down, but choosing premium private-label items, thus improving Kroger's profits.

This week we have talked about a two-speed economy where households with low incomes struggle and reduce their spending, while higher income earners, though reducing their savings rate, are still able to keep up with their style of living.

Kroger's earnings also help us understand better how consumers are behaving. As a matter of fact, when we see categories such as "Fresh" or "Our Brands" that outperform, we can infer that consumers are cooking more at home, thus hurting restaurants. When consumers are cost-conscious, Kroger usually experiences a favorable setup. But, as always, a favorable macroeconomic environment doesn't necessarily imply that a single retailer will do well: competition in this sector is high, and gaining even a tiny fraction of a percentage in market share means millions for companies of the scale of Kroger.

Kroger's Q3 2025 Earnings Review

With the setup we describe, the market was expecting Kroger to report a beat. Well, its results are mixed. On revenues, the company reported $33.86B, coming in below estimates by $320M. However, it managed to achieve an adj. EPS beat by $0.02 to $1.05. So, we are seeing in one day Dollar General move up by 10% while Kroger loses 7% at the time of writing.

Let's unpack the report to see where the issues occurred. Identical sales ex-fuel increased by 2.6%, while analysts expected 2.9%. This alone was enough to trigger a re-rating of the stock. Gross margin increased by 40 bps to 22.8% of sales, showing the positive effect of the divestiture from the specialty pharmacy business.

Kroger also informed us that it bought back shares aggressively under an accelerated program worth $5B, announcing that it will spend another $2.5B during Q4. Surely, the company's dry powder can now take advantage of the share price drop caused by the earnings report. However, the company's leverage ratio increased from 1.21 to 1.73 in one year. This is because, through 2024, the company issued more debt. Kroger targets a net total debt/EBITDA ratio of around 2.4x, which means the company will probably take on new debt to fund its share repurchases. The consequence is that we are seeing higher interest rate expenses that have almost doubled in one year. In Q3 Kroger paid $146M, while in Q3 of last year it paid $86M.

I understand that companies can leverage their balance sheet and use their financial structure to return capital to the shareholders. But, even though companies can choose to do so, I wonder why Kroger couldn't truly decide to invest this capital in lower prices to attract more traffic. True, the company's net income margin is thin (1.9%), but even adding a few basis points as an investment to attract more traffic could pay off. Kroger did indeed hint that its sales won't grow as fast as first promised because it will battle for price affordability, but just by not taking on new debt, it would have the millions to invest in this fight without increasing the risk of its balance sheet. I am not completely against debt, of course, but I think it is unwise when companies take on new debt mainly to fund share repurchases. True, share repurchases help the company's EPS, but paying less interest would too.

Now, Kroger updated its FY guidance, and investors didn't read what they expected. Instead of seeing it raised higher, Kroger is narrowing its ranges, tilting to the low end of the guidance, the most important metric: ID sales. While we were expecting the company to grow ID sales at 3.05% at the previous midpoint of the guidance, we now have a midpoint of 2.9%. This is news that investors aren't liking. Everything else is confirmed, but retailers are top-line companies. If volumes don't perform as people expect, then the stock will see its multiples compress.

Kroger's Valuation

After having more than doubled in five years, the stock is retracing back from its ATH of $75 reached this summer and now trades below $62. The fwd PE is 13.8, and the fwd EV/EBITDA is 7.9. These are low multiples, especially when we look at other retailers. Leaving aside Costco and Walmart, which trade at tech-like multiples, even discount stores such as Dollar General and Dollar Tree trade at an earnings multiple in the high-teens if not the low-twenties. Given Kroger's profitability and growth metrics, barely above 2%, these multiples make sense. On profitability, the company scores well because of its high return rates, which makes me wonder even more why the company is borrowing cash for buybacks instead of investing its organic cash in its business. However, its margins are thin and this clearly compresses the earnings multiple that investors are willing to give to the company.

All in all, I think this last quarterly report marks a setback for a company that was seeing positive momentum. Whether this will be just a one-time incident or the start of a new negative phase remains to be seen. Q4, in this regard, will prove much more important. It is usually the strongest quarter for grocery retailers, and it will allow us to see how Kroger is doing. A quarter ago, I considered Kroger a buy. In case the business had remained intact, I would have written that the post-earnings drop is an opportunity. But this time, I think the market rightly reflects a deterioration of the outlook and thus compresses the multiple. As a result, I am downgrading Kroger from buy to hold and suggest monitoring Kroger's Q4 earnings to better understand how things are unfolding.

data.title: Mixed Growth And Deflationary Pressures: 3 Calls For Asia
data.description:
By Deepali Bhargava, Regional Head of Research, Asia-Pacific and Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

ING's base call: A weaker growth outlook for Asia

While recent trade agreements signal a de-escalation in tensions, they offer limited assurance that export growth will remain robust. The reduced tariff gap between China and its regional peers stands out as a clear strategic win for Beijing. However, most of ASEAN, except Singapore, now face 19-20% tariffs. The full impact of this is yet to be seen on export growth, which has been rather resilient in 2025. Softer exports and weaker growth are likely to remain headwinds for several regional currencies. Countries with strong exposure to AI-related exports may outperform, but for others, the agreements do little to counteract the broader slowdown in trade momentum.

We expect GDP growth in Korea and Japan to accelerate in 2026 as substantial fiscal stimulus filters through to household consumption. In contrast, downside risks have intensified for domestic demand-driven economies such as India and Indonesia in the near term, which should leave overall growth in Asia weaker compared to 2025. India’s inability to secure a trade agreement with the US remains a key concern unless negotiations progress. Meanwhile, Indonesia’s growth outlook is vulnerable to investment headwinds stemming not only from tariff-related uncertainty but also from subdued government spending.

Our risky call: Deflationary pressures persist across the continent

While unfavourable base effects may cause inflation readings to edge higher next year, we expect the key drivers behind the slowdown this year to persist.

First, consumer spending across Asia remains subdued despite easing inflation, as labour markets continue to show weakness. Discretionary spending is likely to stay constrained, reflecting a K-shaped post-Covid recovery that has deepened income disparities at the lower end. This dynamic points to continued softness in core inflation, as weak demand limits businesses’ pricing power.

Second, fuel prices are likely to remain benign, supported by stable supply conditions and moderate global demand. This will further alleviate cost pressures across transportation and manufacturing sectors.

Third, persistent overcapacity in China is widening the demand-supply gap in Asia, exerting downward pressure on prices. This deflationary trend is likely to continue spilling over into regional markets.

Our bold call: Government incentives could boost the Korean won

Korea's economic growth is projected to accelerate to 2.0% YoY in 2026 (vs 1.2% in 2025), supported by government spending and steady export performance. Continued strong global demand for AI chips is expected, while the weak KRW is expected to help offset some of the adverse effects of the 15% US tariffs. Should demand for semiconductors exceed expectations, GDP growth in 2026 could grow even further to 2.3% YoY.

With inflation close to 2%, a faster closure of the negative output gap, and little property price stabilisation, the Bank of Korea may end its easing sooner than expected, possibly leading to stronger-than-expected KRW appreciation.

The government also intends to increase incentives for equity market investment, enhance shareholder rights, and improve corporate governance to support Korea's goal of attaining developed-market status. These initiatives, combined with solid large-cap performance (primarily from leading Korean exporters) and enhanced accessibility to the Korean FX market, could attract further capital inflows into Korean asset markets while tempering outbound investment activity by domestic investors.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: What The 50-Year Mortgage Debate Reveals About U.S. Housing Constraints
data.description:
By Julia R. Cartwright

The US housing market in late 2025 is defined by contradictory forces: rising prices but slowing growth, increasing inventory but falling affordability, and a demographic shift that is weakening long-run demand even as short-run supply remains structurally constrained.

Against this backdrop, President Trump’s proposal for a 50-year mortgage is an attempt to stretch affordability in a market that has outpaced incomes, and it exposes deeper issues. Mortgage duration is both a financial feature and a policy artifact shaped by decades of government intervention dating back to the New Deal. A 50-year mortgage may expand access by lowering monthly payments, but it also dramatically increases lifetime interest costs and could raise prices depending on supply elasticity. The debate over this proposal is ultimately a debate over the real frictions in the housing market, namely interest-rate lock-in, constrained supply, and the institutional architecture that prevents solutions like portable mortgages from being widely available.

The American housing market rarely changes in sudden leaps. Prices adjust gradually, construction responds slowly, and mortgage product design barely shifts at all. That is why the mere suggestion of a 50-year mortgage by President Trump is so economically revealing. If housing finance policymakers are floating half-century debt structures, something fundamental in the market is out of balance.

Today’s housing market presents a strange tableau: home prices are still rising, but at a slowing pace. According to the latest Federal Housing Finance Agency (FHFA) data, prices are up roughly 2.2 percent year-over-year in Q3 2025. Sales have ticked up modestly, with existing home transactions rising 1.2 percent in October. Inventory is finally improving, up about 12.6 percent year over year, driven mostly by new construction rather than existing homes. Mortgage rates have eased from their peaks and now sit in the six-percent range for many buyers. On paper, this resembles a soft landing, but in reality, the market remains defined by broad affordability stress.

Many homes are sitting unsold for long stretches, not because buyers are absent but because sellers are holding out for pandemic-era prices. Renters’ expectations of becoming homeowners have collapsed from 52.6 percent in 2019 to just 33.9 percent today, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. And demographic headwinds are emerging: births are declining, population growth is slowing, and long-run demand will weaken as the nation ages. Housing should be cooling naturally, yet it isn’t. The affordability crisis is so acute in the short run that policymakers are reaching for financial engineering solutions rather than addressing structural constraints.

It is amidst this backdrop that Trump proposed the 50-year mortgage, casting himself in the lineage of major housing finance reforms, much like President Roosevelt’s role in ushering in the modern 30-year mortgage during the New Deal. By extending loan terms, the administration argues it can meaningfully lower monthly payments and open the door to homeownership for buyers priced out of today’s market. In that narrow sense, the idea appears palatable; when affordability is collapsing and buyers are increasingly constrained by monthly cash flow, stretching the mortgage horizon looks like an intuitive policy lever. But as with any major change in mortgage design, the economic logic is more complicated.

A longer mortgage lowers monthly payments at the cost of paying much more interest over many more years. For a median-priced $415,000 home purchased with an FHA loan at 3.5 percent down, here is how the math works:

Mortgage Term Interest Rate Monthly Payment Number of Payments Total of All Payments Total Interest Paid
15-year 5.5% $3,272.21 180 $588,998.69 $188,523.69
30-year 5.99% $2,398.48 360 $863,451.30 $462,976.30
50-year 6.4% $2,227.44 600 $1,336,462.35 $935,987.35

The 50-year mortgage trims the monthly payment relative to the 30-year, but at the cost of doubling the total interest burden. For households focused solely on monthly cash flow, particularly first-time buyers, this tradeoff can appear worth it. A lower payment either gets a family into the home they want or allows them to buy a more expensive house. Economically, it functions like any intertemporal tradeoff: more affordability now, much higher cost later.

Critics argue that introducing ultra-long mortgages will push home prices higher. The answer is that it depends entirely on supply elasticity. In markets where new housing is constrained by zoning, permitting delays, or not-in-my-backyard (NIMBY)-driven land use restrictions, extended mortgage terms can, in fact, capitalize into higher prices. In elastic markets, the effect is muted. This is not a moral failing of the policy; it is simple microeconomics. If your policy goal is to increase homeownership, you accept certain tradeoffs, just as every country with 40- to 100-year mortgages has.

What often gets lost in the discussion is that the United States did not adopt the 30-year mortgage because markets naturally arrived at it. The product is fundamentally the outcome of government intervention. During FDR’s New Deal, the Federal Housing Administration standardized long-term amortized mortgages, displacing the short-term, interest-only loans that had dominated before the Great Depression. Fannie Mae later expanded liquidity and uniformity in mortgage finance. The 30-year mortgage was authorized by Congress in the late 1940s and eventually became dominant because federal agencies guaranteed it.

In other words, the “normal” American mortgage is not a market creation; it is a political one. A 50-year mortgage would simply be the next step in an 80-year continuum of policy-driven mortgage evolution.

The deeper issue in the housing market is not the absence of exotic mortgage products. It is that existing homeowners are frozen in place. Millions of households locked in 2-4 percent mortgage rates during the pandemic. With current rates near six percent, these homeowners don’t want to move, even when downsizing or scaling up might make sense. That keeps existing inventory off the market. Meanwhile, a record share of homes for sale are new construction, not existing properties.

One innovative solution would be portable mortgages, where the borrower could keep their existing mortgage rate but shift the collateral to a new home. Instead of being trapped in their current house because of a pandemic-era 3 percent mortgage, a household could sell, buy a different property, and simply move the lien from House A to House B. In theory, this would dramatically improve mobility, unfreeze existing home inventory, and loosen one of the tightest bottlenecks in the current housing market: interest rate lock-in.

But portable mortgages do not exist in the United States for reasons deeply rooted in the structure of American mortgage finance. The US system is built around long-term, fixed-rate mortgages that are pooled into mortgage-backed securities, financial instruments priced according to the specific borrower and the specific property at the moment the loan is issued. Letting borrowers carry their old loan to a new house would upend that securitization model, causing investors to absorb unknown collateral risk midstream and making the securities far harder to price.

The dominance of the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage compounds the issue. If borrowers could port low rates across multiple moves, they would have little reason to refinance, starving lenders of the fee income and interest rate resets they depend on to originate new loans. Investors could also face greater duration risk, being stuck earning three percent for decades even as market rates rise, causing them to demand higher rates across the entire mortgage market. And unlike countries such as the UK or Canada, where portable mortgages are common, US mortgages are secured by a specific property for the life of the loan and fixed for thirty years, not two to five.

Changing collateral midstream would require new appraisals, new legal filings, and a fundamental reengineering of mortgage securitization. All of this means that while portable mortgages could meaningfully improve housing mobility, they run directly counter to the incentives and infrastructure of the US mortgage system. Banks and investors prefer refinancing into higher rates, and the legal plumbing is built around property-specific collateral, not borrower-specific contracts. As a result, portable mortgages remain economically appealing in theory but institutionally implausible in practice.

If, however, banks and regulators could find a way to make portability compatible with the existing system, it could be a genuine game changer for American homeowners. Imagine a world where a young couple who locked in a low rate on their starter home is not punished financially for having a third child and needing more space, or where empty nesters can downsize without watching their mortgage payment jump simply because they move. Portability would make the mortgage contract follow the household’s life cycle rather than anchor it to a single property, smoothing mobility across labor markets, helping people move closer to better jobs, and reducing the mismatch between housing stock and household needs.

This would mean more efficient use of the existing housing stock, less pressure to overbuild in certain markets, and a healthier, more dynamic relationship between housing and labor-market mobility. It is precisely because the gains to households and to the broader economy are so large that portable mortgages are worth serious experimentation, even if the institutional and regulatory hurdles are high.

Ultimately, the debate over 50-year mortgages is less about exotic loan structures and more about the deeper structural limits of America’s housing system. Affordability has deteriorated because supply is constrained, mobility is frozen, and our mortgage architecture has not evolved with economic realities.

Extending mortgage terms may offer short-term relief, but the real innovations, like portable mortgages or reforms that unlock supply, require rethinking the institutional plumbing that has defined US housing finance since the New Deal. If policymakers want lasting affordability rather than financial patches, they must address the structural forces that make such extreme proposals politically viable in the first place.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Hormel's Strong FY2026 EPS View: Is It Enough To Break The Downtrend?
data.description:
Intro

I think it’s fair to say that the majority of food sector companies haven’t given investors much reason to celebrate this year. The explanations aren’t hard to find—consumer choices and behaviors are shifting. Part of this reflects a secular move toward healthier foods and away from highly processed products, while another part will hopefully be more temporary, linked to inflation-driven pressures affecting consumer purchasing decisions. Adjusting to these new realities has created uncertainty across the sector, which has also shown up in the financial results.

Hormel Foods Corporation’s (HRL) case is a bit trickier to unpack. On one hand, the company appears relatively well positioned for the secular trend toward more healthy and protein-based products, given that much of Hormel's portfolio leans in that direction, and with its turkey segment standing out as the clear bright spot, the alignment looks pretty good with these underlying shifts. On the other hand, a significant portion of its offerings still remain in more processed categories, where consumption is broadly trending lower.

Adding further complexity, Hormel is currently in the midst of a leadership transformation announced in July of this year. Given how dynamic the current marketplace changes are, highly focused management definitely plays a key role in how well a company adapts to the current landscape. From an investor’s standpoint, the leadership transition in these challenging times provides an additional layer of risk to Hormel’s story.

With Hormel’s fiscal Q4 2025 earnings just released (the company’s fiscal year runs from October to October), and with full‑year FY2025 results and key FY2026 guidance now on the table, I’ll focus on breaking down these results and outlook to capture the freshest insights, while briefly touching on Hormel’s broader story.

After completing this analysis and listening to management’s earnings call, I rate Hormel as a Buy. However, it is a cautious buy, given the risks tied to marketplace changes and the fact that the FY2026 outlook provided by management was not a kind of blowout, but rather cautiously mixed—somewhat better on the EPS front but slightly weaker on revenue.

The stock’s favorable reaction to the results may have been driven less by the outlook itself and could be seen as a relief rally, reflecting that the market had expected something worse. As highlighted in the valuation chapter, I see a justified stock price in the range of $22–25.

Whether these results and the outlook are enough to reverse the stock’s downtrend will only become clear over the longer term, as in my experience, such powerful trends do not reverse quickly, and the process of bottoming and building a new uptrend takes time. Until the stock finds firmer footing, patient investors will meanwhile be rewarded with a solid dividend, which is well backed by solid cash flow and Hormel's low debt level.

Hormel in Brief

Hormel presents their story:

Hormel’s story begins in 1891, when George A. Hormel founded Geo. A. Hormel & Co. in Austin, Minnesota. It started as a small meat‑packing business and gradually grew into one of America’s most recognizable food companies.

Today, the company generates approximately $12 billion in annual revenues, with 62% coming from retail, 32% from foodservice, and 6% from international markets.

Hormel Foods began trading on the New York Stock Exchange in 1990.

Today, the company employs approximately 20,000 people, operates more than 30 production facilities across the United States, and is headquartered in Austin, Minnesota.

Recent Dynamics

Stock Price

While the stock appeared to have bottomed and held the $28 level through the first part of the year, weak Q3 earnings at the end of August broke that support, driving shares down to nearly $21 by late October. Since then, the stock has trended upward and was positioned right on the 50‑day SMA line ahead of the Q4 earnings release.

Breaking the $24 level and staying above would build stronger momentum to begin forming an upward trend, though the technical picture suggests that the bottoming process will likely take time.

Leadership Change

As is often the case when a company’s stock price plummets and stakeholders grow dissatisfied, a leadership transition becomes highly likely. Hormel’s board of directors followed the course, but took an unusual step by bringing back company veteran Jeff Ettinger as interim CEO while also promoting John Ghingo to President—effectively appointing two leaders to run the company.

Ettinger previously led Hormel from 2006 to 2016, while Ghingo was serving as executive vice president of the Retail business unit prior to his appointment to a new role. Under the refreshed structure, John Ghingo will oversee all three core business segments—Retail, Foodservice, and International—as well as global operations, supply chain, R&D, and strategy. During his 15-month interim tenure, Jeff Ettinger will oversee corporate functions. including finance, legal, HR, communications, and compliance. These management changes took effect on July 14, 2025.

The chairman of the board shed light on their decision:

“John brings modern brand-building skills essential in today’s food landscape,” said Newlands. “Jeff’s understanding of our business and stakeholders makes him the ideal interim CEO.”

The permanent chief executive officer is expected to be appointed in October 2026, marking the conclusion of this planned Hormel Foods leadership transition.

Q4/FY25 Results

Hormel reported Non‑GAAP EPS of $0.32, topping estimates by $0.02. Revenue came in at $3.20 billion, missing expectations $3.22 billion by $20 million.

Q4 and full‑year revenue growth came in at 2.3% and 2.5%, respectively, driven primarily by strong performance in the foodservice segment, which saw acceleration in Q4 with growth of 6.5% and 5.5% over the same periods. The international segment recorded a quite sharp revenue decline in Q4, down 5.6%.

Outlook

The outlook provided was mixed: the estimated EPS midpoint of $1.47 is above analysts’ expectations of $1.44, while the revenue midpoint of $12.35 billion is slightly below the consensus estimate of $12.45 billion.

Dividend & Cash Flow

While the FY2026 dividend increase was modest at just $0.01, to $1.17, it still marked the 60th consecutive raise. The company’s FY2025 free cash flow of $634 million nearly matched the dividend payout of $633 million.

With a robust balance sheet—nearly $700 million in cash and short‑term investments, $2.85 billion in long‑term debt, and a net debt/EBITDA multiple of roughly 1.7 — the dividend is well secured.

Valuation

In my view, Hormel’s protein‑centric and well‑diversified portfolio, its scale and leadership position in the sector, combined with modest growth and a strong balance sheet, warrant a conservative P/E of at least 15–17. Based on the FY2026 EPS estimate of $1.47, this translates into a fair value range of $22–25 per share.

Risks

In my view, Hormel’s main risk lies in execution. With the food sector undergoing a secular shift, execution must be at a very high level to successfully navigate this dynamic environment. Hormel’s current unusual twin‑leadership structure appears somewhat fragile from that perspective, at a time when the environment requires laser‑focused management.

A second, more significant risk is ongoing cost inflation, which may last longer than currently projected.

Takeaway

While somewhat justified, I think the market has been overly aggressive in discounting certain packaged food processors, almost as if their future growth potential has been written off entirely. Conagra (CAG), which I also analyzed recently, along with Hormel, has been particularly under pressure. Yes, consumer behavior is changing, and it is reasonable to factor in additional related risks, but especially in Hormel’s case, its protein-centric portfolio deserves recognition, as it is in many respects also a beneficiary of this environment.

That said, a great deal of work certainly remains to be done to fully adapt to the evolving marketplace. I believe Hormel has all the levers in place to regain a firmer footing, and investors have meanwhile a good opportunity to invest in a premium company at a modest valuation with a strong and secure dividend.

data.title: Global Defense ETFs: Mapping The Opportunity
data.description:
By Roxanna Islam, CFA, CAIA

One of the most persistent themes this year has been global defense. Historically, it has been an industry that tends to shine when geopolitics drives the macro narrative. But 2025 has shown that even in the absence of a single headline “shock,” investors subtly grew interest in global defense. Funds like the Select STOXX Europe Aerospace & Defense ETF (EUAD) and Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD) have sat near the top of the U.S.-listed non-leveraged ETF performance tables for most of the year. Now ETF issuers have rushed in with more targeted tools, launching six more global/international defense ETFs in 2025.

Why defense? (More details here.) Defense companies offer exposure to rising security and defense budgets during ongoing geopolitical tensions. For investors, that can translate into resilient cash flows, long-term contracts, and a potential hedge against geopolitical shocks in a broader equity portfolio.

Why international/global defense? (More details here.) U.S. defense has already had a strong multi-year run. Traditional broad ETFs like ITA now sit on double-digit YTD gains and several billion dollars of inflows. The more interesting story in 2025 has been how quickly non-U.S. budgets are catching up. Spending growth across Europe and Asia has grown as allies modernize and expand their militaries. That diversification can reduce single-country risk while increasing exposure to fast-growing defense budgets and differentiated regional players.

Global Defense ETFs Quietly Gaining Attention Since 2023

Performance and flows of several existing ETFs have carried the rising momentum of defense.

In Europe, HANetf’s Future of Defence UCITS ETF (NATO LN) accumulated over $2.8 billion in assets since its launch in March 2023.

In the U.S., SHLD has seen some significant attention since its launch in September 2023. This ETF has returned 62% YTD. And even more impressively, the ETF has the highest net inflows out of its peer group, at $3.4 billion YTD. That’s even higher than the $3.2 billion seen by the largest player, iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA).

Launched in October 2024, EUAD quickly became one of the top-performing non-leveraged ETFs in the U.S. ETF universe. Year-to-date, it is up around 64% due to concentrated exposure to European names like Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBY), BAE Systems (OTCPK:BAESY), and Saab (OTCPK:SAABY). Assets have grown to roughly $1 billion in only around a year.

2025 in Defense

After the success of SHLD and EUAD, it’s not a surprise that many issuers decided to launch global defense funds. Some of these have carved very specific niches in the Asian and European markets. Below is a brief description of these funds (listed in order of launch date).

  • The PLUS Korea Defense Industry Index ETF (KDEF) is arguably the most unique of the group, given that it focuses on a single country. In contrast, broad Korea ETFs like the iShares MSCI South Korea ETF (EWY) are heavy in technology (EWY consists of 40% semiconductors with less than 3% in aerospace & defense). From a thematic perspective, KDEF’s value is targeting under-represented defense names with significant growth potential, rather than depending on the typically prevalent tech story. Since its launch in February 2025, the fund is up 86%.
  • The iShares Defense Industrials Active ETF (IDEF) is an active play on the industry which focuses on companies positioned to benefit from increased global defense and security spending. In practice, active sector ETFs are often useful if you want access to a broader view and/or shifts in a theme that is not limited to a specific sector or industry. Note that aerospace & defense is traditionally an industry that’s part of the broader industrials sector, so holdings tend to be very specific in an indexed fund. According to the iShares website, IDEF uses proprietary AI-powered models, alternative datasets like defense revenue exposure, and qualitative insights from BlackRock’s specialists.
  • The WisdomTree Europe Defense Fund (WDEF). WisdomTree recently released a suite of three global defense ETFs. WDEF, which aims to capture the “structural multi-year re-armament cycle in Europe” is the largest with $27 million in assets. Its holdings must generate at least 10% of their revenue from defense-related activities (although it uses an exposure score to derive weight from those holdings with greater percentages). Relative to EUAD, WDEF holds a wider breadth of stocks and is less concentrated, although top names are similar. From a geographic perspective, the fund is allocated to 31% UK, 28% France, and 16% Germany.
  • The WisdomTree Asia Defense Fund (WDAF) is broader than KDEF and targets Asian defense in countries like India (currently 43% of weight), South Korea (36% of weight), and Japan (9% of weight). Note that China is not included. Exposure to Asian defense can complement U.S. and European exposure, especially given Asia’s tech innovation in cybersecurity and other defense technology.
  • The WisdomTree Global Defense Fund (WDGF) is the broadest of the WisdomTree defense group and includes exposures to the U.S., Europe, and Asia. Over 50% of the fund’s weight is in the U.S. However, it provides the opportunity to tilt toward regions with accelerating defense investment. It can serve as a core defense holding.
  • The Tema International Defense Innovation ETF (GDFN) is also an actively managed fund which focuses on defense innovation, including companies that build and manage cybersecurity systems, use AI and big data, and build advanced military systems, robotics, etc. for defense applications.

Bottom Line:

Demand for defense investments, particularly in the growing global/international market, has allowed ETF issuers to carve out several niche areas into new opportunities for investors in 2025.

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2025. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit www.vettafi.com

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bally's: A Value Trap At $17, Years Away From Planned Expansions
data.description:
• Even at $17, Bally's (BALY) is a value trap, with 19 casinos in 10 states. For $6 a share more, you can buy Caesars (CZR) with 50 properties and more than 65 million rewards members.
  • Near $10b in new projects announced (Chicago, New York, and Las Vegas), and a net debt of $3.72b, Bally's already has GPLI REIT financing its $1b Chicago project. More debt is likely as we near these sizable project starts.
  • Investors may be enticed by the prospect of buying 19 casinos in 11 states generating $2.4b TTM in revenues at $17, but the prospects for $10b expansion in already crowded markets don't work now.
  • Existing properties can't generate significant FCF to reduce debt over a reasonable duration.

A bit of history for context is necessary in a Sell call now and a watchlist advisory by 2Q 2026.

The origin of Bally's as a public gaming company goes back into the 1970s, when the company was a manufacturer of slot machines and arcade games. After several challenges by gaming regulators, it received a casino gaming license in New Jersey for its 1980 Park Place casino, renovated from the base of the historic Dennis in Atlantic City. Park Place, built off a strategy of focusing on slots, budget players, and location feed from nearby Sands, made tons of money for the parent, Chicago's Bally Manufacturing Corp.

I joined the company as Senior VP of marketing operations in 1988, responding to an offer from its then President and CEO Richard Gillman, a former stockbroker involved in the original IPO. Gillman presided over a tight, profitable operation until his strategy failed as AC matured in the late 80s. Bally's hired me to oversee a tough transition to keep Bally's Grand (Golden Nugget) high-end play (bought from Wynn (WYNN) in 1987). At the same time, they wanted to add mass play using the Bally's formula. It was heavy stress on rewards for slot players, with little effort pursuing VIP.

The slot machine business nationally did well in that era of expanding legal gaming in ever more states and tribal outlets. But overall, among Bally's key strengths was its determination to keep debt workable in terms of cash flow. Analysts who liked Park Place stock were always concerned about debt.

Financing moves, like the acquisition in 1987 of Wynn's (WYNN) Golden Nugget, was easy now. Out of the blue came an arcade game first - a video game called Pac-Man and later Ms. Pac-Man. A variable from a Japanese game, Pac-Man exploded from day one in fall 1980, reaching $1B in sales. (Note: the manufacturing business and casinos were separately traded stocks.)

By the 1990s, Bally's Pac-Man arcade game alone reached $2.5b in revenue. It became a cultural phenomenon. By 2016, its accumulated revenue since introduction had hit $14b. This tidal wave of cash was the fuel that enabled it to grow. Ultimately a sluggish stock price in 1990 attracted trucking entrepreneur Arthur Goldberg, and finally it was bought by current Asian hedge fund operator Soo Kim's Standard General. To the industry, the Bally assets were deemed meh, per se, but Kim had a wider vision clearly sprung from the history of the company before him. It was the operating assumption that the Bally's brand had resonance with the gambling public. He paid Caesar's (CZR) $20 for the brand name. He has since applied the goal to bring Bally's up to tier-one casino chains by infusions of debt.

That view was not shared by industry leaders, and there was virtually no interest among top-tier casino operators in adding Bally's properties. That's how Soo Kim, with limited assets, scooped it up.

Above: Chicago project. Already crowded market in a difficult urban setting.

Enter Soo Kim: A hedgie from Asia's Standard General fund with under $671m in AUM, he began accumulating $20m of Bally stock in October 2020. What he saw in Bally's was the long tail of a company struggling out of a manufacturing sector to gain a footprint in casinos since 1980. And to an extent, post-Pac-Man, it went bumping along at the second-tier chain with a nice footprint in Vegas, with the rebranded Horseshoe it acquired from Caesars. By February, Soo Kim completed the transaction by paying $4.6b for total control. The company had a long legacy of a transactional mentality, which fit Soo Kim's goals.

From the get-go, the plan was to resurrect the existing casinos at bargain prices, resurrect its once more visible brands, and use debt to finance expansion. The bet is that investors in the sector see the value of 19 casinos, solid operations, cats and dogs included, as far more than $17 now. In January of this year, the stock reached a high of $19.70.

Knowing the company for more than 39 years, I don't see a rationale for its significantly gaining market share in existing markets. And its three mega projects planned are all located in high oversupply metro markets.

There are investors who believe BALY can hold its own and find real value at $17, but I don't at this time. The brick and mortar casino sector will be fine toward 3Q 2026 or earlier, but until then, there are too many headwinds to endure for a heavily indebted, tier-two casino group. Most of the properties are located in mature casino markets with short-term growth stymied by current macro headwinds.

Above: Past initial bullish outlook.

The perceived value at $17 is weak vs. a buy of Caesars at $23. BLY has 7m members vs. CZR's 65m, for example.

The presumed growth engine is in the very early stages of development. Bally's won the license for downtown Chicago, now financed by the Gaming & Leisure Properties REIT (GLPI) at $1.1b. At the earliest, I see accretive EBITDA not rolling into the parent until the early 20230s.

So what's driving investors now to either sit on their Bally's positions, open new ones because it appears cheap, or accumulate and price average? My view holds that it's a weak case for a hold now with so many opportunities for buy on the dip moves for companies like Caesars.

1. BALY's existing properties are entirely dependent on the recovery of the sector. At best it can inch up while others in the market increase shares.

2. I believe the financial picture is challenging. It can hold shares at a second-tier level, throw off minimal EBITDA, and slowly reduce debt. That's a long tail trek at the end of which can't promise much upside.

3. If any of the three big projects makes it to a debut by 2030, we could see a far more interesting upside. But that's five to seven years away. Until then, the $17 is kind of dead money vs. other real bargains in casino stocks.

4. If Bally's appears to have bet right on its long-term plans, it could begin to look interesting at some point. For example, the Chicago project becomes accretive to earnings by 2030. That's why it makes sense to watchlist the stock before that debut. Winning one of the three downstate New York licenses for the Bronx project enters a market that will be swelled up with a mega-resort built by another New York bid winner, $20b net worth hedgie Steve Cohen, as part of his NY Mets MLB holdings in Queens. Add to that an expanded Genting Queens racino (the third winner) and hotel long in place backed by deep-pocketed Genting of Asia, and finally New York's existing casinos: MGM Yonkers and Genting's Queens racino, and you would have a vastly underserved market for the Bally's Bronx project to compete with.

Bally's plan for a mixed-use casino and residential development on the site of the always troubled Las Vegas Tropicana confronts the same big question: Why? Does Vegas need more casino space? Savvy investor Tillman Fertitta had a grand plan for a Vegas project he has now abandoned. He keeps his 12.3% position in Wynn shares because of its wider scope, including Boston and, in development, the Al Marjan property on the Arab Gulf Coast.

Bear in mind when first announced, the entire industry pounced with bids for New York downstate, including Wynn, Las Vegas Sands (LVS), and MGM Resorts (MGM). After the initial enthusiastic rendering of fabulous mega proposals, one by one they withdrew, leaving the three final bidders with approval. It was, in truth, like getting a participation trophy in Little League for the three winners.

Summary: Our thesis is simple: $17 slipping down or inching up in the same price range at this time in the sector is dead money. It's at best a second-tier casino operator, short on funds - but still able to access partners like GLPI. A strong recovery of the sector in 2026 will be a plus with a small upside. My view now is that there are far superior assets in the sector available at bargain prices, some of them close to Bally's $17.

My call is to sell now before a negative quarter or two drives the shares below $12 if the recovery is stalled. Then watchlist the stock as the Chicago project nears completion to tease out what will be bullish estimates for its debut. Also bear in mind that under certain circumstances, a sell-down of the company's underperformers could be an option to raise cash. So it's a watchlist stock at that point.

For now, move the Bally's money to CZR for far better potential returns.

data.title: Richtech Robotics: Political Catalysts And A Lot Of Hope
data.description:
Richtech Robotics (NASDAQ:RR) made news with a big 18% jump on Dec. 3rd following news stories claiming that the Trump administration is going to put robotics firms in focus. Commerce Secretary Lutnick has been privately meeting with robotics companies, and the market believes that Richtech Robotics is going to be one of the winners in this policy shift.

The big news is that the administration is considering an executive order, much like what it did for the nuclear power industry earlier this year. That was a great catalyst for many names in the industry, especially small and mid-cap companies. We're seeing the same thing in RR now that robotics are in view.

The US Wants to Take On Robotics

The impetus for this political will in boosting robotics is in competition with the Chinese manufacturing sector. China has a significant number of robots compared to the US (some estimates say easily double, if not triple) and has factories that are so automated they keep the lights off, since the robots use infrared cameras ("IR") to "see" and humans watch from IR in a separate building when they need to check on the factory. They call these "dark factories." I recommend this quick Wall St. Journal video about them if you'd like a primer.

We already have some factories that operate close to human-less in the US. Take Tesla's (TSLA), Nevada facility, which operates 90% autonomously, but nothing at the same scale as some Chinese factories. This makes it difficult to compete in the sector, and is one of the reasons that manufacturing in the US has been declining for some time.

It's impossible for me to believe that we're serious about "bringing manufacturing back to the United States" until we fix these statistics. Our production hasn't meaningfully changed in twenty years, and our capex today is below that of the 90s.

We will see if this new executive order and potential legislation is meaningful to these statistics, but that will take time. The timelines shown above are immense, and span decades.

This isn't a problem that was created in a day, nor is it solvable in one.

It's unclear to me if we're capable of solving this in the long run. Even if this administration is successful in their efforts, it will take several successive administrations, all focused on the same goal, in order to actually achieve real change. Time will tell on that front.

What is clear is that solving the manufacturing stagnation in America cannot be done without robotics and automation. Advanced manufacturing like semiconductors requires care and precision, and assisting specialized humans requires a lot of grace, things that many bots of the past lack.

Enter Richtech Robotics

RR is one of the firms building this new generation of advanced robots, with its hands in both industrial and commercial ventures currently. Robots-as-a-service is projected to be a major industry, though it will likely have few winners. Investors see RR as one of those potential winners, because they are reaching into multiple industries with generalist robots.

While I am skeptical that much of the stock is built up on hype, the actual business seems very real, albeit small.

The flagship robot that has already been deployed is a drink mixer, more or less. ADAM is the "AI-powered bartender, barista, and boba maker that wows your customers and boosts your bottom line," according to RR's website. I am not all too impressed with these kinds of robots, mostly because I see these kinds of robots as showpieces and not real game-changers for businesses.

Maybe I'm wrong, but the TAM for people who want a robot bartender to serve them drinks (more than just at a one-time event where it's a novelty) is fairly small compared to industrial needs.

Here's where things get interesting for me, the industrial bots.

The company's newest humanoid robot is called Dex, and it is supposed to offer firms a whole different kind of automation experience. It was debuted in late October this year, and has not seen any real deployment as far as I can tell. It's likely too soon to have fulfilled orders, to be fair.

They boast that Dex "fits into the systems you already use." This is the major selling point for many manufacturers and industrial firms that have struggled to use legacy automation tools and robots, because they were typically too bulky and too hyper-specialized (or else they were not cost-effective) to plug straight into a business. Entire factories needed to be refitted and rebuilt, causing time delays and imposing costs that many firms simply refused to shoulder.

Humanoid robots like Dex promise to fix this dilemma, which is the largest selling point for them.

Here are Dex's specs. Note that it doesn't use IR like many legacy robots or those that work in dark factories, but instead a combination of sensors using "real" vision, i.e., RGB and depth cameras.

While Dex and ADAM are on much of the marketing on their website, RR builds many kinds of robots. The one I am most interested in is their other industrial robot, Titan, built to assist humans (or robots like Dex) by delivering things across factories with many moving parts. These are similar to the kind of robots Amazon (AMZN) uses in its warehouses, but Titan's ability to navigate complex and constantly changing paths with humans in them could be a game-changer for many businesses.

Here is RR's full lineup:

Current deployments are very small. There is a BMW (OTC:BMWKY) dealership in Plano, Texas that they have deployed Titan at. RR has a promotional video on the deployment. They call it a "case study" on their website, but it's 2 minutes long, so I refuse to repeat that in any seriousness.

Otherwise, it seems like most of the deployments so far have been to one-time corporate events. While good for exposure and investor interest, RR has been unable to materially affect its bottom line yet with robotics deployments.

That's expected, since the company is in its early stages, but it is definitely something to consider when investing now. We are not at the point yet where they can claim large-scale success in deployment.

A Classic "Innovation Co" By The Numbers

I have long had a wishy-washy relationship with "innovations cos," or firms that make (and even deliver!) new and high-tech products/services, but fail to do it profitably. This combination often leads to stock price charts that look like rollercoasters, because their valuation is largely built on hope for the future and a promise of future earnings, not current earnings growth.

Here's RR's price and EPS since it came to the market a few years ago; they are a perfect example of this market dynamic.

Revenue is not growing, and operational expenses are very high. Again, this is classic innovation co territory, and I don't expect them to become more profitable for some time. Economies of scale needs to take place, and more robot-as-a-service deployments allows for more depreciation, which would improve the bottom line.

Where is the money coming from to cover all of this? The answer is unfortunate for current shareholders, but usually worthwhile for most firms to engage in, especially when their stocks are as bid up and hyped as RR's has been: stock issuance.

As the company needs money, they have been issuing shares to cover those costs. This comes at the expense of current shareholders, which are diluted by the issuance of these new shares. Consider that since its Jan. 2024, RR has more than tripled its share count.

That being said, I believe that for innovation cos like RR, this is often better than taking on debt. This is something that I like about RR's balance sheet; they are very low on liabilities. Very little long-term debt, which was mostly spent on their new Nevada facility.

Their liabilities to assets ratio is very low, meaning that they may be able to sustain their firm for a long time. They have been working to reduce liabilities in general, down from $4M to less than half that today.

A solid cash runway is vital for the survival of these firms, which need to outlast their competitors and last long enough in the market so that the massive spending they are doing on R&D pay off. R&D takes time, and many speculative ventures like robotics firms can't last long enough for it to pay off.

In a company like this, we should expect to see that kind of R&D spend, but without profits to fund it, keeping tabs on its level is important.

Calculating the actual runway they have is difficult because it will cost Richtech very little to dilute shares and raise more cash at any time. This is a good problem to have, but note that it makes estimating how long their cash will last a game of cat and mouse.

From their last 10-Q, here is what we can expect:

Cash & Equivalents: $32.893M

Short-term Investments: $52.616M

That brings our core liqudiity to ~$85.5M.

Annualized operating expense total $14.9M, extrapolated from a 9-month trailing operating expesnes of $11.197M.

Based on these current numbers, Richtech looks to have a little over two years in OpEx covered by their cash, or five years if also using their short-term investments. Adding in growth-oriented capital expenditures, which total $4.86M, lives us with a four year runway.

Of course, this is likely to change over time, as firms at this stage often do not have steady expenses or revenue. However, the current figures paint a good picture. Not a lot of firms this size have four years of their core business expenses on hand, and fewer have that and less than their liabilities in assets.

Conclusion

It's always difficult to rate companies that operate as a loss because the real power behind the stock is currently narrative and technical, not fundamentally driven. There is a lot of promise in Richtech Robotics, both from the company itself and from the government, regarding potential interventions that would benefit the robotics market and thus the company.

However, almost all of that is currently hype and hopeful foresight. That can be very powerful, but also result in heavy losses. The stock's history should be very clear on that point. Investors also run the risk of further stock dilution, which could cause shares to be devalued, even if they are correct that RR becomes more profitable over time.

Buying RR today is a bet that it will become profitable in the future, because there are no earnings to speak of today. It's a bet that its products will be useful for re-industrialization efforts in the US. It's a speculative bet, and one that could go very poorly. RR has a long road ahead of it, but one that is worth watching.

For now, I am giving the firm a hold rating, but I am adding it to my watch list and will keep tabs on it. The stock is too bid-up currently, especially since the most recent jump was based mostly on rumors and the anticipation of future political intervention. Without any details on that intervention, the buying pressure is mostly hype.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Microsoft: Ignore The Anti-AI Chatter
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) was leading all hyperscalers in the first half of 2025, like I said it would in my initial coverage of the stock.

Unfortunately, it lost its lead in the second half of 2025 as Alphabet (GOOG) took over as the clear leader, outperforming the hyperscaler complex for the full year.

The thing is that, since we launched our portfolio in late September, The Pragmatic Optimist Portfolio, we have allocated the highest amount of capital to Microsoft, investing over 12% of our initial capital in the stock.

However, the stock has been under pressure since its Q1 earnings amid a shift in the “AI trade” narrative in November as well as growing competitive concerns over Google’s latest Gemini 3 model launch.

Not only that, two days ago, OpenAI (OPENAI) announced "code red" to improve the quality and reliability of ChatGPT, especially as Google's Gemini models have leveled the playing field. FYI: Microsoft is OpenAI’s largest external investor with a 27% stake in the company.

To make matters even more complicated, news emerged yesterday that Microsoft has lowered sales quotas for their AI products after several sales staff missed their goals in the fiscal year that ended in June, per The Information.

While Microsoft management issued a statement denying the accuracy of the report, it has undoubtedly ushered in uncertainty amid “AI bubble” fears. There is no doubt that OpenAI’s success is important for Microsoft’s business strategy. However, Microsoft has been diversifying by investing in rival AI labs like Anthropic (ANTHRO) and Mistral AI, while developing their own in-house AI models and custom silicon. Finally, there is no reason to become bearish on OpenAI just because they announced “code red.”

The truth is the short-term volatility in stock prices is just a function of the changing narrative. When it comes to the AI landscape, narratives can flip very fast. In this case, I continue to remain bullish on Microsoft as an existing investor over a 3-year investment horizon. Having said that, I may be trimming my position sizing in the stock at certain levels in order to better manage risk in my portfolio.

Microsoft’s Q1: What AI Capex Slowdown?

Out of all the hyperscalers, Microsoft had the most muted price action in its stock price after its Q1 FY26 earnings at the end of November, despite beating both its top and bottom lines.

During the quarter, Microsoft Cloud revenue surpassed $49B, growing 26% YoY, with their commercial RPO (Remaining Performance Obligations) up 50% YoY to $400B with an average duration of 2 years.

Out of this, Microsoft Azure and Cloud Services revenue grew 40% (100 basis points higher than the previous quarter). This is compared to a 34% YoY growth rate in GCP (Google Cloud Platform) and a 20% YoY growth rate in AWS (Amazon Web Services) (AMZN). However, investors were not particularly impressed, as management projected a slight slowdown in their Azure growth rates next quarter to 37% YoY.

Note, since the last time I wrote on Microsoft, we got the much-needed clarity into Microsoft and OpenAI’s new agreement that was released before the Q3 earnings, where Microsoft will now own 27% of the new OpenAI PBC (valued at $135B), with a $250B Azure commitment and commercial rights to OpenAI models extended through 2032.

What was also striking was that Microsoft management, who had earlier projected for their pace of capex growth to slow in FY26, did an about-turn, where they now expect their FY26 capex to grow at a faster rate than FY25. In Q1, Microsoft reported capex of $34.9B, with analysts projecting full-year capex to exceed $120-$125, especially after taking the $250B commitment from OpenAI into account. As I discussed in my previous post, Microsoft’s CFO Amy Hood has made it clear that she is not particularly focused on picking a date when revenue growth and capex will converge and instead is focused on building a backlog and delivering capacity to meet their clients’ workload requirements.

Breaking down OpenAI’s Code Red

With Microsoft projecting their capex at a faster rate in FY26 than the previous year, let us not forget that Microsoft remains OpenAI’s largest external investor with a 27% stake in the company. In simple terms, this means that OpenAI’s success is extremely important for Microsoft’s overall business strategy.

So, when OpenAI’s CEO Sam Altman announced “code red” two days ago, investor pessimism further deepened in Microsoft stock, which had already been under pressure in the month of November from a combination of a general worsening of sentiment in the AI trade along with Google releasing their new Gemini 3 models that received much praise from the market.

Now with OpenAI announcing “code red,” it is fairly obvious that the main reason is attributed to rising competition from Google’s Gemini, especially after notable industry leaders.

I also wanted to share a couple of charts from the Financial Times, which show that Gemini app users may be more engaged than those of other rival chatbots, while Gemini’s app seems to be catching up to ChatGPT’s.

Note that these charts are from before the Gemini 3 launch. Not to mention, Marc Benioff’s (CEO of Salesforce (CRM)) statement that he is switching from ChatGPT to Google’s Gemini 3 created quite a stir.

I want to make it clear. It’s not like I don’t believe that OpenAI can come out of this stronger. However, what makes matters slightly complicated is that OpenAI’s financial plans involve tremendous levels of spending on AI infrastructure of $1.4T in data center and infrastructure commitments over the next 8 years. This is obviously not the case with Google, which is sitting on $75B in cash, more than OpenAI has raised in its entire existence.

Analysts at HSBC project that the company will need to raise an additional $207B by 2030 to sustain operations. While OpenAI expects to grow their revenues from an estimated $20B in 2025 to over $100B in 2029, any hint of a potential failure to reach those targets will create massive risks of not finding enough funding, which could create ripple effects in the AI ecosystem.

Microsoft Management Denied Having Lowered AI Sales Quotas

The good news is that Microsoft has been diversifying away from its exclusive reliance on OpenAI by investing in rival AI labs like Anthropic and Mistral AI, as well as developing its own in-house AI models and doubling down on its custom silicon roadmap.

Like I said in my earlier post, when OpenAI went on its deal-making spree in September with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Nvidia (NVDA), Oracle (ORCL), CoreWeave (CRWV), and more, with the Financial Times tallying it up to $1T+ worth of deals, Microsoft was missing from the party. The way I saw it, it was a strategic move on Microsoft’s part to expand and diversify their customer base beyond just catering to OpenAI’s gargantuan computing needs.

However, yesterday, news emerged that Microsoft had reportedly lowered how much salespeople are expected to grow annual sales of Foundry and other AI products after several sales staff missed their goals in the fiscal year that ended in June, per The Information.

While the stock naturally declined on the news, Microsoft issued a statement where they denied the accuracy of the report soon afterwards, stating that the story “inaccurately combines the concepts of growth and sales quotas, which shows their lack of understanding of the way a sales organization works and is compensated.

Revisiting My Valuation

While OpenAI’s “code red” does bring a layer of uncertainty, despite Microsoft diversifying its investments across AI labs while doubling down on its custom silicon roadmap, forward growth estimates have been revised higher over the last month.

On the revenue front, Microsoft is expected to grow its FY26 revenues by 16.01% YoY, and growth rates are expected to remain more or less anchored at these levels over the next two years to generate a total revenue of $435B.

Meanwhile, when it comes to earnings per share, expectations have also been revised higher, with growth rate at par or slightly higher than revenue growth over the next three years, as can be seen below.

What I would also like to point out is that Microsoft’s free cash per share has started to inflect higher on a TTM basis despite growing capex, which is generally a leading indicator of forward stock price returns.

The stock is currently trading at 30x its forward earnings per share estimates. Note that this is the same as that of Google, which has seen its price-to-earnings multiples expand over the last few months. But what is important to note is that Microsoft’s earnings per share are expected to grow at a faster rate than that of Google’s.

Assuming Microsoft’s price-to-earnings multiples expand to 36, which is roughly 2x that of the average S&P 500, given the growth rate in its earnings per share over the next 3 years, it should translate to a price target of $635 per share, representing an upside of 33% from current levels.

My final verdict and conclusions

Microsoft is the victim of a change in narrative in the AI landscape at the moment. While OpenAI’s success is important to Microsoft’s business strategy given its capex projections, I believe Microsoft is strategically diversifying across rival AI labs as well as doubling down on its own custom silicon roadmap to better cater to their enterprise customers’ AI workloads.

It is hard to predict how long the period of uncertainty will last. On a technical level, it is key for Microsoft to hold its 100 DMA, which will allow it to find support on a short-term basis.

Meanwhile, the underlying fundamentals remain intact.

As a result, I will reiterate my Buy rating on MSFT stock with an updated price target of $635 per share, representing an upside of 33% from current levels.

data.title: A Weaker Dollar As The Fed Moves Toward Neutral: 3 Calls For FX
data.description:
By Chris Turner, Global Head of Markets and Regional Head of Research for UK & CEE

ING's base call: Less restrictive Fed policy delivers softer dollar

Our baseline call sees the Fed cutting rates back to neutral somewhere near the 3.00/3.25% area. With the ECB not touching its policy rate during 2026, FX hedging costs for eurozone holders of US assets should fall further. Three-month hedging costs have already fallen to 1.85% per annum from 2.45% as recently as July and are expected to fall further. Increased hedging activity should weigh on the dollar as it did in April this year.

For reference, the recent ECB Financial Stability report highlighted that eurozone investors, as of the second quarter of 2025, held $3.8tr of US equities, $800bn of US sovereign debt and $1.5tr of other US debt securities. With debt investors typically dealing with lower prospective returns, it will largely be this community which we expect to raise dollar hedge ratios next year.

Our risky call: Risk of negative real rates hurting the dollar

If political pressure mounts on the Fed to cut rates, we will all definitely be talking more about real interest rates in the US turning negative again. Two-year real swap rates have dropped to just 0.75% now from a peak of 2.5% in 2023. Any suggestion that the Fed is taking rates to inappropriately low levels could see real US rates turn negative again and weigh on the dollar.

For reference, when the Fed attempted to reflate the economy after the pandemic, real rates were taken down to the -3% area, and the dollar was under broad-based pressure. Were US real rates to make it back to the -1% area in 2026, we could see the dollar some 5-7% lower from current levels.

Our bold call: QE would be a game-changer for the dollar

Were the nexus of the US Treasury and the Fed to somehow agree that yield curve control was a good idea, the resurgence of quantitative easing would weigh heavily on the dollar. Printing money to keep the long end of the Treasury market in check, while good for growth, would further damage the credibility of the dollar and lead to a much clearer return of the ‘risk on, dollar off’ trading environment seen in the decade immediately after the Global Financial Crisis.

In the absence of a crisis, we presume the ECB and the Bank of Japan would be operating with much more stable balance sheets than the Fed and that the euro and the yen would witness some meaningful outperformance against the dollar. Under this policy, the US administration could achieve two of its goals at the same time: stable funding levels for the US government and a weaker dollar.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
